title: Serving HTML
subtitle: Serve a real web page from your server

sections.content:
So far your server has returned plain text and JSON. But when you visit a real website, you see styled pages with headings, images, and layouts — not raw text.

That's because real web servers serve **HTML files**. HTML is the language browsers understand for displaying rich, formatted content.

Instead of writing HTML inside your JavaScript code (which gets messy fast), we'll put HTML files in a special folder and tell Express to serve them automatically. This is called **serving static files** — files that don't change based on the request, they're just served as-is.
keyPoints: Static files are served directly without any processing
The public/ folder is a convention for files that should be accessible via the browser
Express can serve an entire folder of static files with one line of code
This is how real websites serve CSS, images, and HTML pages

sections.instruction: First, create a `public` directory inside your project folder. This is where your static files (HTML, CSS, images) will live.

troubleshooting.solution: That's fine! The folder already exists. You can continue to the next step.

troubleshooting.problem: I'm not sure I'm in the right directory
troubleshooting.solution: Run 'pwd' to check your current directory. You should be inside your project folder (the one with app.js and package.json). If not, use 'cd' to navigate there.

sections.instruction:
Create an `index.html` file inside the `public` folder. You can create this file using a text editor — save it as `public/index.html` in your project directory.

This will be the homepage of your server.

codeBlocks.code:
Welcome to My Server

This page is being served by Node.js and Express.

I built this myself.

troubleshooting.problem: I'm not sure how to create the file
troubleshooting.solution: Option 1: Use a text editor (VS Code, TextEdit, Notepad) — create a new file, paste the HTML, and save it as index.html inside the public folder. Option 2: Use the terminal — you can use 'nano public/index.html' (Mac/Linux) to open an editor right in the terminal.

sections.question: What is the purpose of `res.sendFile()` in Express?
options: It uploads a file to the server
It sends an HTML file as the response to the browser
It deletes a file from disk
It reads a file into a variable
sections.explanation: `res.sendFile()` reads an HTML file from your server's disk and sends its contents as the HTTP response. The browser receives the HTML and renders it as a web page. This is how servers serve web pages.

sections.instruction:
Now tell Express to serve files from the `public` folder. Open `app.js` and add this line **near the top of your file**, after the `const app = express();` line but **before** any of your `app.get()` routes.

This one line makes every file in the `public/` folder accessible through your server.

troubleshooting.problem: I'm not sure where exactly to put this line
troubleshooting.solution: Put it right after 'const port = 3000;' and before your first app.get() route. The order matters — Express checks static files first, then routes.

sections.instruction:
Stop your server (**Ctrl+C**) and restart it, then visit **http://localhost:3000** in your browser.

Instead of the plain text "Hello, World!" you should now see a **styled HTML page** with a heading and paragraphs. That's your `index.html` being served!
sections.expectedOutput: A styled web page with the heading "Welcome to My Server" and two paragraphs

troubleshooting.problem: I still see the old 'Hello, World!' text
troubleshooting.solution: Express checks static files first. If public/index.html exists and the static middleware is set up correctly, it should override the app.get('/') route for the root URL. Make sure: 1) The file is named exactly 'index.html' (not 'index.HTML' or 'Index.html'), 2) It's inside the 'public' folder, 3) The express.static line comes BEFORE your routes, 4) You restarted the server.

troubleshooting.problem: The page loads but looks like raw HTML code
troubleshooting.solution: The file might be saved with a .txt extension instead of .html. Make sure it's saved as index.html (not index.html.txt).

sections.prompt: To serve static files from a folder called 'public', you add this middleware: `app.use(express._____('public'))`
sections.hint: The Express middleware function for serving files that don't change.
sections.successMessage: That's it — express.static() serves files directly from a folder without any processing.

completionMessage: You're now serving a real HTML web page from your server! Any file you put in the public/ folder — HTML, CSS, images, JavaScript — becomes accessible through your server. This is how real websites work.
